summary:
The RIGHT-2 trial did not evaluate prehospital transdermal glyceryl trinitrate for stroke; there’s zero documentation supporting its study or effectiveness. Moreover, nitrates carry significant dangers—such as hypotension and increased bleeding risk—that make them unsuitable candidates for stroke therapy despite theoretical benefits in other settings.

Final conclusion: Prehospital transdermal GTN had neither proven benefit nor formal evaluation in stroke care according to available data. Thus, asserting its effectiveness in the context of the RIGHT-2 trial is incorrect.

Answer: \boxed{\text{No}}  
Justification:

There is no record of the RIGHT-2 trial assessing prehospital transdermal glyceryl trinitrate for stroke. Additionally, nitrates increase the risk of severe hypotension and may exacerbate cerebrovascular injury during acute stroke events—a known absolute contra-indication per current neurology standards—which makes therapeutic use both unsafe and unproven.
